extracts.extract: Investigative journalism focuses on one particular clear angle that can be proven or disproven through in-depth research and careful documentation. It focuses not on isolated cases but on systemic failures and recurrent models, in order to establish the underlying causes, win justice for victims, and put an end to violations.

When a source provides a journalist with information that may spark an investigation, they must ask for detailed information. How did this happen? Where? When? Why? Who are the main people involved? If the answers given are convincing and logical, there might be an idea there. If not, it is no more than a rumour.

Government documents are a treasure trove of information. They include sources as diverse as the official gazette, the company register, parliamentary minutes, stock market announcements, property and land registries, court judgements, tender announcements, and lists of those who have been granted or stripped of citizenship.

Civil society organisations' reports and databases provide a wealth of fresh, unpublished information that can produce excellent results. Such organisations tend to be very interested in cooperating with the media, with whom they enjoy a mutually beneficial relationship: the media makes use of their data, and they benefit from press coverage of their activities.

Through digital analysis of videos made publicly available, BBC Africa's investigative unit was able to establish the time and place of the killing, the weapons that were used, and the identity of the perpetrators, confirming that the Cameroonian army was responsible despite its repeated denials.

When a journalist gets an idea for an investigation they need to answer: Is the idea clear and does it have a particular angle? Has it been investigated before? What is the new angle? How important is it to people? Are the sources publicly available? Is the idea risky? Will proving it be difficult? What budget, skills, and tools are needed?

The journalist needs to make sure that the idea is specific rather than general and that it deals with a single issue. We have to think outside the box to get a narrower angle. Brainstorming questions can lead us to preliminary research that in turn allows us to develop a specific idea.
During preliminary research, the journalist needs to find out whether this story has been covered before. If it has been done, then they rethink the angle or move on to other ideas.

Every idea for an investigation involves some risk, be it legal, physical, or digital, and the journalist needs to be aware of what that risk is and how best to avoid it. If they conclude that they would be risking death by investigating, the best thing is to stay away. No story is worth dying for.

The hypothesis is critical to how an investigation proceeds. It must have a solid grounding in carefully acquired facts and draw on preliminary research showing that there is a story worth writing. A hypothesis is made up of facts and assumptions. Facts means solid, corroborated and carefully documented information, while assumptions means information that is yet to be corroborated.

Investigative journalism, like science, is about coming up with hypotheses, testing them, and trying to prove them. The best examples of investigative journalism are rooted in a hypothesis that allows them to work out what happened, how it happened, and why it happened.

Hypotheses make it easier to collect data, gather and organise new facts and evidence, and analyse it. They help keep control of the investigation, test the best methodology, focus and be precise, establish the boundaries and goals, set budgets and keep a tighter hold on time and resources, and establish the sources of the investigation.

A hypothesis can be tested, is based on established facts as well as uncorroborated assumptions, is concise, is coherent, and deals with a single problem. Although the hypothesis is central to the investigative process, it can always be amended if new evidence and facts require. A good journalist should always be open to evidence that contradicts their hypothesis and work just as hard to disprove it as to prove it.

After coming up with a hypothesis, a journalist can work out what information they have already and what information they want to get access to. The best way to do this is to produce a written research plan. A systematic plan requires systematic thinking: questions about the facts, sources, opinions and analysis, the background, and anticipated obstacles. The plan specifies the facts necessary, the questions that lead to those facts, the sources that answer these questions, the methodology, and acceptable standards for evidence.

The investigation plan schema maps each point of the hypothesis to specific questions and the sources that can answer them. This helps the journalist predict results, decide where to start looking, map out prospective and confirmed sources, establish sufficient standards of proof, and decide on methodology, budget and timeline.
extracts.notes: The handbook provides a detailed worked example with a Points/Questions/Sources table

extracts.extract: When producing an investigation plan, we focus on proving that there has been logical, legal and ethical wrongdoing and establishing where this occurred, who is responsible and what their motives were (money, fear, coercion, maintaining the status quo, or neglect and incompetence).

Reliable sources are the backbone of any investigation. They are the gateway to facts, to background and context. They feed into one another, lending one another credence. A good journalist makes their sources work for one another. The number of sources is proportional to the strength of an investigation: the more sources an investigation cites, the stronger it is. Diverse and numerous sources make an investigation more balanced and objective.

A good journalist begins their research with open source materials. They gather, analyse and assess information, perhaps creating a special database to facilitate new discoveries and conclusions that they can then try and corroborate using their own network or any of the other sources available to them.

Paper sources provide strong evidence or a historical or contextual background. They are entirely reliable so long as not forged and cannot be modified or refuted in court unlike human sources whose narrative may change. But they may be protected by privacy laws. Paper sources sometimes require expert analysis and explanation (budgetary or accounting documents, for example, or court judgements).

Primary sources are people directly connected with the event: victims, eyewitnesses, people responsible, people who intervened or participated. All primary sources will have different stories despite being closest to the event. Every source has their own biases, their own personal, psychological and social makeup that affects their view of events. Some might lie, give unsubstantiated suggestions, exaggerate, miss out things, or conflate facts and assumptions.
Journalists should never be afraid to ask: How do you know? Examining information carefully is our first responsibility.

The ideal human source in investigative journalism is the closest person to the story who can be relied upon and trusted, who is willing to stand by their statements publicly and who is easily and safely accessible. Journalists should look for human sources that meet all these criteria at the same time.

Experts suggest interviewing victims, those hurt by an event or enemies of the parties under investigation first, because they are most likely to talk. Sources that are the object of suspicion or accusation will talk to a journalist when they realise that everyone else involved in the event either has talked or might talk.

By communicating with sources, journalists aim to collect facts and get access to documents and supporting evidence. They should ask directly: do you have any documents that support your story? A meeting should also aim to acquire new sources: who knows about this other than you? Who was with you? Please give me their names and how to contact them. This is a central part of building a network of sources.

A good journalist knows that sources conflate facts and assumptions, and seeks the help of experts in analysing documents and interpreting events. Experts typically provide unbiased, in-depth and detailed information. A good journalist can also get sources to talk honestly. The approach taken when interviewing victims is totally different from the approach used when interviewing those responsible, and different again from experts.

Anonymous sources should only be used for a clear and justifiable reason, when there is no other option, and with the agreement of all parties. If you use an anonymous source, you must confirm the information using another source. Mark Hunter says that information should never be attributed to an anonymous source unless it is confirmed by at least four other sources.

When sources request anonymity, journalists should establish whether they do not want their name or job title published, whether they do not want the information itself published, or both (off the record). You should ask who else is aware of the information. If there are several people, ask for their names and contact details without divulging the original source. You can then publish the information and note it has been confirmed by various sources.

Digital sources -- government and private databases, websites, video and audio libraries and social media -- constitute a vast archive that, with analysis, can help you get at the facts. They provide rapid, cross-border information. But digital sources produce soulless investigative reporting if they are not supplemented by human sources close to the issue. They also require careful verification, because they are easy to modify and decontextualise.

In investigative journalism evidence must be proven beyond all doubt, because doubt will be interpreted to the detriment of the hypothesis. If an investigation presents possibilities rather than facts, then it is not yet fully ripe. A good journalist works to develop suspicion or possibility into certainty. Certainty requires the elimination of any doubt, and doubt can only be eliminated by marshalling solid, reliable evidence.

A good journalist looks for evidence refuting their hypothesis. This is the best way of avoiding guesswork. Contradictory evidence may demonstrate that the event did not happen as suggested in the hypothesis, and that the error was not deliberate. Evidence may prove that the error was the result not of collusion and bad intentions but dereliction of duty or neglect. This is a very different story, even if the ultimate result is the same.

Witness testimony must be provided by primary sources directly linked to the event, by several different and independent sources who cannot have decided on their story beforehand. For any piece of information to be acceptable, it requires corroboration by at least two known, independent sources. Several sources may depend on a single original source, and in this case they should all be taken as one and not treated as separate sources for corroboration.

An investigative journalist compares and inspects different accounts in order to work out where they intersect. They should test the credibility of each source throughout the interview, ask for evidence supporting their testimony, try to establish sources' motives, and weigh up information making sure it makes logical sense. A sceptical attitude is one of the best defences against being manipulated.

An investigative journalist should work hard to get access to all the primary sources on both sides of the investigation -- for and against. They should have recourse to independent, neutral third parties familiar with the facts who can help them assess how accurate and trustworthy a narrative is.

Data does not only mean numbers. It means any raw material -- numbers, letters, symbols -- whether it has been processed or not, even if it is simply individual facts obtained in different contexts. To put together a database that will help produce a quality investigation, you need to be aware of where to find that data, whether it is descriptive or numerical, and how best to get at, develop, and verify it.

Much published or available data will fail to totally meet your requirements. You will need to put together a database tailored to your specific needs by using existing databases, collecting the missing data yourself or with the help of others, and conducting surveys, interviews and field visits.

In the Jordanian House of Representatives investigation, the team found government contracts on official websites, tender data from the Public Procurement Bureau, an open database of companies and shareholders, and leaked Civil Status records. Using all this information, the team came up with a spreadsheet format appropriate to the investigation, with names, dates, company details, contract values, and other relevant information. The team proved the investigation by analysing, collating, indexing, and arranging data extracted from publicly available documents.
extracts.notes: ARIJ/amman.net Jordanian HoR investigation case study

extracts.extract: Data cannot take the place of traditional journalism, and even the best data analysis cannot replace fieldwork and investigation. The team submitted 27 freedom of information requests to various ministries, interviewed experts on the constitutional and legal aspects, and confronted representatives who had violated the constitution and the law.

Verifying data protects journalists from misleading the public. Data is at least as dishonest as people: at the end of the day, it is collected and collated by human beings. Data cannot be trusted absolutely, because it is influenced by the biases of those who collect it. You should always verify your data and ask questions to those who collected it.

We advise journalists to ask government bodies for raw data, because analysis of raw data helps produce a successful investigation. It is also often necessary to access information that the government does not want to reveal. In this case you will have to negotiate with officials, work out how to classify it, digitalise it and enter it into a database. You will have to sift through it carefully and review it to guarantee it is free of mistakes.

Official documents used should confirm the incidents and acts that the journalist wants to prove. They should be related to the facts and the characters involved. Documents should not be used simply for decoration.

Leaks that do not come in document form but rather as copy-pasted or encoded text require particular care. Anyone whose name appears can easily dispute or deny their involvement, and journalists may be sued for publishing unsubstantiated information. When no original copy is provided, we have no way of knowing whether sentences or words have been lost or added, or whether figures, dates, or spelling of names is correct.

When power struggles take place between different elites or factions within the regime itself, many documents will be issued, some entirely valid and some less so. Journalists may find themselves being used as puppets in these conflicts. Be particularly careful in these circumstances: publishing a fake document may end your career.

Personal experience means a journalist undertaking an entirely legal action, without concealing their identity, in order to test compliance to the law by others and uncover mistakes or violations. It is one of the most important sources of proof, particularly in investigations involving careful inspection of legal procedures or routine official practice. Eyewitnesses often do not pay enough attention to the detail of legally and procedurally complex processes.

When using personal experience: engage with the system as a normal citizen in an entirely legal way. Repeat the experience multiple times to prove the problem is systemic, not attributable to bad apples. Conduct in-depth research and familiarise yourself with the relevant regulations before engaging. Document everything: ask for receipts, cards and documents, take photos, record films, or write everything down in detail as soon as you finish.

Journalists use lab analysis to confirm that violations have taken place that cannot be proven by other methods. Lab analysis constitutes very solid evidence, especially if produced by a neutral, specialised, recognised party. It requires a precise and solid methodology to rule out any doubt in the results. You should always stick to highly respected labs that enjoy technical and scientific accreditation.

Lab analysis results alone do not constitute an investigation. You also need to establish the background and context, work out what results mean from a scientific perspective, what effect they have on victims, and how the results connect to the victims and the event. The investigation may require testing multiple samples over an extended period to demonstrate that contamination is not an isolated case but part of a systematic and ongoing problem.

Investigations looking into the safety of food, medicine, water etc require taking samples correctly. You might seek the help of a technical expert in taking the samples and record the process on video. You should also record the payment as well as the process of taking samples to the laboratory and handing them over.

In many investigations a journalist will not be able to produce convincing proof on their own because of the technical nature of the issue. You should always choose the best experts -- the ones used by the courts or other official bodies. Get at least two or three independent experts to produce reports and compare results, so as to ensure they are neutral and independent. When finished, they should write a full report and, if possible, sign and stamp it.

Just as with lab analysis, technical expertise alone does not produce an investigation. It is part of the process of proof, and must be supported with context, background, source testimony, and, if possible, documentary evidence.

Going undercover should be a last resort used only after all other methods have failed and you have documented the failure. The information should be of great importance to the public interest. Document all your attempts to acquire information by professional and legitimate means. Always get your editor's approval. Always have a back-up plan in case you or your team are arrested. Consult a specialised media lawyer to establish legal problems that might arise.

The point is for the journalist to show, precisely and objectively, the existence of a serious and systematic issue. Although there is a very thin line between going undercover and entrapment, journalism is not about setting people up to fail. By setting up an ambush you are indirectly encouraging them to make a mistake they would not have made otherwise.

Always give the person or organisation affected by the investigation the chance to respond so that the investigation is fair and just. When producing the story, always make it clear to the public that you went undercover. Be transparent and honest and explain your decision to do so.

When using hidden cameras: the harm of allowing the information to remain hidden should be greater than the harm caused by using hidden cameras. You should have exhausted all other means. The recording should expose major and systematic failings. You should not record in homes or private spaces protected by law. The editor-in-chief should sign off. Consult a specialised media lawyer. Put in place an emergency plan. Always present affected parties with footage and provide a right of reply.

A survey is a kind of research involving collecting and collating primary data from a particular community in accordance with a unified methodology, building on a particular hypothesis and set of questions, before analysing this data statistically using established scientific techniques. Since investigative journalism is a collaborative team activity, a team of journalists and researchers can help put together a survey methodology, gather data and analyse them using statistical software like SPSS.

In Bangladesh, journalists combined survey methodology with in-depth interviews, pictures and documents. Rather than interviewing anyone willing, the sample was selected randomly, with 400 out of 7500 cases. The survey exposed massive corruption in a social health program, showing 96% of users were receiving support they were not entitled to. The investigation lasted twelve months and involved eighteen journalists, producing results that were almost indisputable.
extracts.notes: Bangladesh health program corruption case study

extracts.extract: In both investigations journalists successfully combined surveys with more conventional investigative techniques. Combining techniques bolsters an investigation and makes it more convincing and credible.

Some journalists reach out directly to the public for information after publication, calling for input from their readership and the broader public. Data provided voluntarily by the public needs a great deal of work to verify and corroborate before it is incorporated into the investigation. Gathering testimony like this requires a solid relationship with the audience based on trust rooted in the journalist's independence and professionality.

Data constitutes the raw material that a journalist uses to cast light into darkness, clear up ambiguities and solve apparent contradictions. Investigative journalism and data-driven journalism both engage in in-depth research and sift through information to exclude any impurities (fake news or misleading data). You should be careful to distinguish data from facts: data does not necessarily mean fact. There may be biases in the way data is collected.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age

extracts.extract: When you first start working on an investigation, look up the data already available -- whether official or unofficial -- to answer as many questions as possible before moving on to posing questions whose answers are unknown and coming up with hypotheses. Data will help make your story measurable, allowing you to render your hows as measurable how-muchs.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age

extracts.extract: There are several database programs for handling data: Microsoft Excel (spreadsheets), OpenRefine (data refining), Fusion Tables (verification), MySQL (databases), SOLR and Access (databases). After collating and refining data, you can use descriptive analysis to answer what, who, how, where and when.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age

extracts.extract: Techniques from data-driven journalism that assist investigations include: analysing data taken from social media profiles of perpetrators or influential people to tease out lines of investigation, and accessing data that has been deleted using archiving tools like Internet Archive or the Wayback Machine.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age

extracts.extract: Fact-checking began with the investigation of fabricated photos or decontextualized video clips. But these techniques have got better and their use more sophisticated, creating a space for a new type of open source journalism. These modern techniques can be combined with traditional techniques to produce high-quality investigations.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age

extracts.extract: By comparing Google Maps with the crime scene, the BBC team were able to prove that not Boko Haram but government forces had carried out the executions, and that they had taken place not in Mali but in Cameroon. The video clip was verified and analysed scientifically: the armed men, the place, and the type and source of weapons were all identified.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age

extracts.extract: OpenCorporates contains information about more than 165 million companies and more than 200 million senior employees. You can conduct general and country-specific searches. Because the website uses specific filters, it is easier to establish connections between companies or work out which companies are run by the same CEO.

Investigative Dashboard (OCCRP) provides a massive database containing millions of records of previous investigations, links and public sources, and also makes it easy to contact experts in various fields from around the world.

FlightRadar24 uses airport data and volunteer networks to show the movement of flights in real time. You can get information about flights, flight paths, planes' registration information and the companies they belong to. MarineTraffic is the maritime counterpart for following ship movements worldwide. Track-trace allows you to trace shipping containers around the world using an ID number.

Amnesty International YouTube Data Viewer helps verify potentially fabricated video clips. All you need is the YouTube link. The tool culls data about the video like the original production date, and provides thumbnails that can be entered into Google reverse image search to identify potential original videos.

MapChecking allows you to produce specially drawn maps to estimate crowd sizes -- how many people might fit in a particular area. This is useful for establishing how many people took part in a demonstration or celebration. Using the cursor, you delineate the area and the tool calculates the square kilometres and approximate number of people.

Given how many ways there are of getting information on others, journalists should be careful to protect themselves. OnionShare allows sending data over Tor's secure network using a one-time link. LastPass manages passwords. Signal should be a central part of any news outlet's communication strategy so that messages exchanged with third parties cannot be hacked or seen by others.

For a journalist, a leaked document is the first clue in an in-depth investigation that will involve careful documentation of facts, marshalling primary sources and confronting those responsible -- as well as verifying the documents' authenticity and establishing the motive for the leak.

In 2016, a journalist surfing the Jordanian Constitutional Court website found the Prime Minister had repeatedly responded to court requests after the statutory time limit. Analysis of all judgements showed failures to submit documents on time in four constitutional appeals, that speakers of both chambers had ignored appeals, that two thirds of appeals failed, and 81% of judgements lacked consensus.

In the case of the Panama Papers, the leaks came in the form of documents, passports, emails, signatures which helped confirm their veracity. But none of this means that journalists are exempted from engaging in their own traditional examination and verification of documents that they want to use in their investigations.

The Panama Papers project drew on more than 11.5 million documents making up 2.6 terabytes of data dating from 1977 to 2015. The International Consortium of Investigative Journalists incorporated the data into a database that makes sifting through it and searching it much easier.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age

extracts.extract: In Sudan, BBC journalists were able to collect and review more than 300 videos shot by activists on the ground, allowing them to reconstruct a scene showing that the Rapid Response Forces had fired live ammunition on protesters. In both cases, journalists were able to dispense with traditional methods and with teams on the ground. Thousands of videos shared on social media, carefully verified, were the deciding factor.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age
